topic: What is a Program & How C Works
subject: C Programming

cards.title: What is a program?
cards.body:
A program is a set of instructions you give to a computer.

Think of it like a recipe:
- Recipe = program
- Chef = computer
- Ingredients = data
- Dish = output

The computer follows your instructions exactly — one line at a time, top to bottom. If you make a mistake in the recipe, the dish goes wrong. Same with code.
cards.example:
A program to make tea:
1. Boil water
2. Add tea leaves
3. Add milk
4. Add sugar
5. Filter and serve

In C, each step becomes one line of code.

cards.title: Why C language specifically?
cards.body:
C was created in 1972. It's one of the oldest languages still used today.

Why HCL and other companies test C:
- It's the foundation. If you know C, you can learn any language fast.
- Operating systems (Windows, Linux) are written in C.
- Embedded systems (the chips inside your washing machine, TV remote) run C.
- As an ECE student, the hardware you work with is programmed in C.

C is called a 'middle-level language' because:
- It's close enough to hardware (you control memory yourself)
- But readable enough for humans (unlike assembly language)
cards.example:
Linux kernel — the core of Linux OS — is almost entirely written in C.
Arduino programs? Based on C.
Your college microprocessor lab? C.

cards.title: How does a C program run? (Compile → Execute)
cards.body:
You write C code in a file → computer converts it → it runs.
This happens in 4 steps:

Step 1: WRITE — You type code in a .c file
Step 2: PREPROCESS — Lines starting with # are handled (like #include)
Step 3: COMPILE — Compiler converts your code to machine language (0s and 1s)
Step 4: EXECUTE — Computer runs the machine code, you see output

The compiler is like a translator — it converts English-like C code to binary that the CPU understands.
cards.exam_tip: HCL asks: 'Which file does the compiler produce?' Answer: Object file (.obj) first, then executable (.exe) after linking.

cards.title: Your first C program — every line explained
cards.body:
#include <stdio.h>     // Line 1

int main() {           // Line 2
    printf("Hello!"); // Line 3
    return 0;          // Line 4
}                      // Line 5

Line 1: #include <stdio.h>
  → 'include' means: bring in the stdio library
  → stdio = standard input output
  → This gives you printf() and scanf()
  → Without this, printf won't work

Line 2: int main()
  → Every C program starts running from main()
  → 'int' means main will return a number when done
  → { } are the opening and closing of main

Line 3: printf("Hello!");
  → printf = print formatted text to screen
  → Text goes inside double quotes
  → Semicolon ; ends every statement in C

Line 4: return 0;
  → Tell the OS: program finished successfully
  → 0 means no error
cards.example: Change printf("Hello!") to printf("My name is Joy"); — that's your first edit!
cards.exam_tip: main() always returns int in C. If asked 'what does return 0 mean?' — it means program ended without errors.

cards.title: What is a variable?
cards.body:
A variable is a named box in memory that stores a value.

Think of RAM as a huge shelf with millions of boxes.
Each box has:
- An address (like a box number)
- A name you give it (the variable name)
- A value stored inside

When you write:  int age = 20;
You're saying:
→ Create a box in memory
→ Call it 'age'
→ It stores whole numbers (int)
→ Put 20 inside it right now
cards.example:
int age = 20;       // stores the number 20
float marks = 87.5; // stores a decimal number
char grade = 'A';   // stores a single character

cards.title: The 5 main data types in C
cards.example:
Which to use?
Student marks out of 100 → int
Temperature like 36.6 → float
Your name initial → char
cards.exam_tip: HCL asks size of data types constantly. Memorize: char=1, int=4, float=4, double=8 bytes. (On 32-bit systems)

cards.title: Operators — doing things with variables
cards.body:
ARITHMETIC OPERATORS (math):
  +   addition         5 + 3 = 8
  -   subtraction      5 - 3 = 2
  *   multiplication   5 * 3 = 15
  /   division         5 / 3 = 1  (integer division drops decimal!)
  %   modulus          5 % 3 = 2  (remainder after division)

RELATIONAL OPERATORS (comparison, returns 0 or 1):
  ==  equal to         5 == 5 → 1 (true)
  !=  not equal        5 != 3 → 1 (true)
  >   greater than     5 > 3  → 1
  <   less than        5 < 3  → 0 (false)
  >=  greater or equal
  <=  less or equal

LOGICAL OPERATORS:
  &&  AND    (both true → true)
  ||  OR     (at least one true → true)
  !   NOT    (flips true/false)
cards.example:
5 / 2 = 2  (NOT 2.5!) — because both are int, decimal is dropped
5.0 / 2 = 2.5 — because 5.0 is float, result keeps decimal
5 % 2 = 1  — remainder when 5 is divided by 2
cards.exam_tip: The % operator trick: To check if a number is even, use: if(n % 2 == 0). This is asked very often in HCL coding rounds.

cards.title: Increment, Decrement & Assignment operators
cards.body:
INCREMENT/DECREMENT:
  ++  adds 1    --  subtracts 1

  Two forms:
  x++  (post-increment): use x first, THEN add 1
  ++x  (pre-increment):  add 1 FIRST, then use x

  int x = 5;
  printf("%d", x++);  // prints 5, then x becomes 6
  printf("%d", ++x);  // x becomes 7, then prints 7

ASSIGNMENT SHORTCUTS:
  x += 5  means  x = x + 5
  x -= 3  means  x = x - 3
  x *= 2  means  x = x * 2
  x /= 4  means  x = x / 4
  x %= 3  means  x = x % 3
cards.example:
int a = 10;
a += 5;  // a is now 15
a *= 2;  // a is now 30
printf("%d", a);  // prints 30
cards.exam_tip: Pre vs post increment is the #1 most asked C question in HCL. Practice: int x=5; int y = x++; What is y? What is x? y=5, x=6.

cards.title: if-else — making decisions in code
cards.exam_tip: In C, 0 = false, any non-zero = true. So if(5) runs. if(0) does NOT run. HCL tests this.

cards.title: for loop — when you know how many times
cards.exam_tip: HCL asks: how many times does this loop run? Count carefully. for(i=0; i<5; i++) runs 5 times (0,1,2,3,4). for(i=1; i<=5; i++) also runs 5 times (1,2,3,4,5).

cards.title: while and do-while loops
cards.exam_tip: HCL loves asking: 'What is the output of this do-while loop where the condition is initially false?' Answer: it runs once and prints something.

cards.title: break, continue and switch
cards.example:
Switch without break = 'fall-through'. All cases after the matched one also run!
This is a very common trick question in HCL.
cards.exam_tip: Switch fall-through: if you forget 'break' after a case, it runs into the next case too. HCL asks the output of switch without break almost every test.

cards.title: What is a function and why use it?
cards.body:
A function is a named block of code that does one specific job.
You define it once, use it many times.

Without functions, if you need to find the area of a circle 10 times in your program, you write the formula 10 times. That's messy.

With functions, you write the formula ONCE, give it a name, and call it 10 times.

C already gives you many built-in functions:
  printf()  → prints to screen
  scanf()   → reads input
  sqrt()    → square root
  strlen()  → length of string

You can also make your OWN functions.
cards.example:
Think of a function like a blender:
- You put ingredients in (inputs/parameters)
- It does its job (the code inside)
- It gives you a smoothie back (return value)

cards.title: How to write a function in C
cards.exam_tip: HCL asks: 'What is the return type of main()?' Answer: int. Not void, not nothing — int.

cards.title: Call by Value vs Call by Reference
cards.example:
swap() function only works if you use call by reference.
If you use call by value, the swap happens in a copy and original stays unchanged.
cards.exam_tip: This is asked in EVERY HCL test. 'What is the output after calling this function?' — check if it's call by value or reference first.

cards.title: What is an array?
cards.body:
An array is a collection of variables of the SAME type stored in consecutive memory locations.

Without array: int m1=80, m2=75, m3=90, m4=65, m5=88;  (messy!)
With array:    int marks[5] = {80, 75, 90, 65, 88};    (clean!)

How to access elements:
  marks[0] = 80  ← first element, index starts at 0!
  marks[1] = 75
  marks[2] = 90
  marks[3] = 65
  marks[4] = 88  ← last element, index is size-1

IMPORTANT: Array index starts at 0, not 1.
An array of size 5 has indices 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
cards.exam_tip: Most common mistake and most asked HCL question: accessing arr[5] in an array of size 5 is OUT OF BOUNDS (valid indices are 0-4). This causes undefined behavior.

cards.title: Strings in C — arrays of characters
cards.body:
In C, a string is just an array of characters ending with '\0' (null character).
The '\0' tells C: 'string ends here'.

char name[6] = {'J','o','y',' ','\0'};
// OR simpler:
char name[] = "Joy";  // C auto-adds \0 at the end

The array size must be at least 1 more than the string length
because of the \0 at the end.

"Joy" needs 4 bytes: J-o-y-\0

Common string functions (need #include <string.h>):
  strlen(s)        → length of string (not counting \0)
  strcpy(dst, src) → copy one string to another
  strcmp(s1, s2)   → compare two strings (0 if equal)
  strcat(s1, s2)   → join s2 to end of s1
cards.exam_tip: Never compare strings with ==. Use strcmp(). This is a trick question in EVERY placement test. == compares addresses, not content.

cards.title: What is a pointer? (The simplest explanation)
cards.body:
A pointer is a variable that stores a MEMORY ADDRESS instead of a value.

Normal variable:  int age = 20;    → stores the number 20
Pointer variable: int *p = &age;   → stores the address of 'age'

Two special symbols:
  &  (address-of operator)  → gives the address of a variable
  *  (dereference operator) → goes to that address and gets the value

int age = 20;
int *p = &age;      // p stores the address of age

printf("%d", age);  // prints 20  (the value)
printf("%p", p);    // prints something like 0x61ff08  (the address)
printf("%d", *p);   // prints 20  (value AT that address)
cards.example:
Think of memory as apartments in a building.
- Each apartment has a number (address): 101, 102, 103...
- age lives in apartment 101, value = 20
- p is a notebook that has written '101' in it
- *p means: go to apartment 101 and see what's there → 20
cards.exam_tip: HCL asks: int x=10; int *p=&x; *p=20; printf("%d",x); — Answer is 20. Because *p changed the original x through its address.

cards.title: Pointer arithmetic
cards.body:
When you add 1 to a pointer, it moves to the NEXT element — not just 1 byte.
How far it moves depends on the data type.

int *p;    p+1 moves 4 bytes (size of int)
char *p;   p+1 moves 1 byte (size of char)
float *p;  p+1 moves 4 bytes (size of float)

Example with array:
int arr[] = {10, 20, 30};
int *p = arr;       // p points to arr[0]

printf("%d", *p);       // 10
printf("%d", *(p+1));   // 20  (moved to next int)
printf("%d", *(p+2));   // 30

Array name itself IS a pointer to its first element!
arr == &arr[0]  (both give same address)
cards.exam_tip: Pointer arithmetic is a favourite HCL topic. Remember: pointer+1 does NOT mean address+1. It means address + sizeof(type).
